# Welcome to MRJL

Welcome to the **Mingwan Junior League (MRJL)** — where engineering meets teamwork, and every competition teaches us something new.

::: {.callout-note}
This handbook is your complete guide to MRJL robotics. Whether you're a new member learning the ropes or a returning competitor refining your skills, you'll find everything you need here.
:::

## What is MRJL?

MRJL is a competitive robotics league where teams design, build, and program autonomous and driver-controlled robots to compete in game-based challenges. More than just building robots, MRJL teaches:

- **Engineering thinking** — breaking problems into solvable pieces
- **Teamwork** — collaboration across roles and responsibilities
- **Iterative improvement** — testing, failing, and trying again
- **Documentation** — recording your journey for judges and yourself

## Our Core Values

| Value | What It Means | In Practice |
|-------|---------------|-------------|
| **Collaboration** | We succeed together, not individually | Pair coding, shared building, team reviews |
| **Iteration** | First isn't final; final is never done | Test early, fail cheap, improve constantly |
| **Modularity** | Build small, connect smart | Design components that work independently |
| **Respect** | Every role matters | Builders and coders equally important |

---

# Section 1: Welcome to MRJL {.unnumbered}

## 1.1 Club Rules

Every member agrees to the following when joining MRJL:

### Attendance & Commitment

1. **Be on time** — Meetings start at the scheduled time. Being late affects the whole team.
2. **Communicate absences** — If you can't make a meeting, notify your captain at least 24 hours in advance.
3. **Come prepared** — Review the previous session's notes before each meeting.

### Safety & Workspace

1. **Safety glasses on** — Always wear safety glasses when cutting, drilling, or testing motors.
2. **Clean as you go** — Return tools to their designated spots after use.
3. **No testing without supervision** — Autonomous code tests require a mentor present.

### Team Conduct

1. **Support your teammates** — Offer help before being asked.
2. **Own your mistakes** — We learn more from failures than successes.
3. **Respect all roles** — Builders, coders, drivers, and notebookers are all essential.

::: {.callout-warning}
Violations of safety rules will result in immediate removal from the activity until a mentor review.
:::

## 1.2 Meeting Schedule

| Day | Time | Activity | Location |
|-----|------|----------|----------|
| **Monday** | 18:00–21:00 | Open Lab (building & coding) | Workshop |
| **Thursday** | 18:00–20:00 | Strategy & Practice | Workshop |

### Weekly Rhythm

**Monday** — Deep work session

- New members: Training exercises
- Returning members: Active build/coding sprints
- Captain: Check-in with each role

**Thursday** — Integration & Review

- Driver practice (30 min per team)
- Autonomous code testing
- Notebook review & updates

## 1.3 Team Commitment

Joining MRJL means committing to:

### Time Requirements

- **Minimum**: 4 hours/week during competition season
- **Preferred**: 6–8 hours/week for competitive teams
- **Tournament days**: Full day availability required

### Competition Commitment

1. Attend all scheduled tournaments
2. Arrive 1 hour before official start time
3. Stay until awards ceremony (unless excused by captain)
4. Participate in load-in and load-out

::: {.callout-tip}
## Commitment Levels

**Casual** — 4 hrs/week, attend 1 tournament
**Competitive** — 8 hrs/week, attend all tournaments
**Leadership** — Above + captain/mentor duties
:::

## 1.4 Team Roles

Every team member holds **two roles** — one **pit role** and one **field role**. This ensures everyone contributes to both robot operations and team management.

### Pit Roles (Robot-Side)

| Role | Count | Responsibilities |
|------|-------|------------------|
| **Builder** | 2x | Mechanical construction, maintenance, modifications |
| **Coder** | 2x | Programming autonomous routines, driver assists |

### Field Roles (Competition-Side)

| Role | Count | Responsibilities |
|------|-------|------------------|
| **Driver A** | 1x | First 30 seconds of driver period (autonomous transition) |
| **Driver B** | 1x | Second 30 seconds of driver period (endgame focus) |
| **Captain** | 1x | Strategy, time management, judge interviews |

### Role Expectations

#### Builder (2x)

- Arrive knowing the current robot state
- Can explain any mechanism's function
- Maintains a "build log" of changes made

#### Coder (2x)

- Version controls all code in the team repository
- Documents autonomous routines with comments
- Tests code before every meeting

#### Driver A (First 30s Specialist)

- Masters the transition from autonomous to driver control
- Excellent spatial awareness for quick pick-ups
- Practices the same 10-second sequence 50+ times

#### Driver B (Endgame Specialist)

- Focuses on high-value endgame actions
- Efficient with endgame time (last 15 seconds)
- Communicates clearly with Driver A

#### Captain

::: {.callout-note}
The Captain does NOT drive. Their job is to maximize the team's performance across all areas.
:::

- **Pre-match**: Strategy brief to drivers, equipment check
- **During match**: Time keeper, timeout manager
- **Post-match**: Notes for notebook, debrief to team

## 1.5 Collaboration

### Pair Coding

Two coders work together at one computer:

1. **Driver** — Types code, manages files
2. **Navigator** — Reviews code aloud, catches bugs, thinks ahead

```
Session Structure (45 min):
├── 5 min: Navigator explains what the code should do
├── 30 min: Pair coding
├── 5 min: Both review the final code
└── 5 min: Commit to repository with clear message
```

### Pair Building

Two builders work together on the same mechanism:

1. **Primary** — Operates tools, makes cuts/drills
2. **Spotter** — Holds parts steady, checks measurements, watches safety

::: {.callout-warning}
No builder operates power tools without a spotter present.
:::

## 1.6 Iterative Development

We follow a **build-test-learn** cycle:

```
┌─────────────┐
│  DESIGN     │ ← Sketch on paper first
└──────┬──────┘
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│  BUILD      │ ← Quick prototype (cardboard is fine)
└──────┬──────┘
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│  TEST       │ ← Does it work? Does it break?
└──────┬──────┘
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│  LEARN      │ ← What worked? What didn't?
└──────┬──────┘
       │
       ▼ (repeat)
```

### The 70% Rule

> "If a design works at 70%, ship it and iterate."

Perfect is the enemy of good. Get something working, then improve it.

## 1.7 Parallel Building

When time is limited, we work on **multiple subsystems simultaneously**:

### Example: Robot Build Timeline

| Time Block | Builder A | Builder B | Coder |
|------------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| Hour 1 | Chassis assembly | Intake prototype | Driver controls |
| Hour 2 | Drive motors | Intake refinement | Autonomous v1 |
| Hour 3 | Mounting | Test intake | Sensor integration |

### Communication Protocol

- **Slack channel**: `#build-status` for real-time updates
- **Design doc**: Shared Google Doc with current specs
- **Handoff notes**: Brief message when switching tasks

## 1.8 Modular Design & Small Steps

### Modular Design

Build components that can be:

1. **Tested independently** — Does the intake work without the arm?
2. **Replaced quickly** — Can swap the claw in under 5 minutes?
3. **Documented easily** — Clear function and interface

### Small Step Iteration

**Instead of:** Build whole robot → Test whole robot → Debug everything

**Do:** Build intake → Test intake → Build arm → Test arm → Integrate → Test whole robot

### The "Hello World" of Mechanisms

Before any mechanism goes on the robot, demonstrate it can:

1. Complete its basic function (claw closes)
2. Withstand normal use (doesn't break)
3. Be quickly attached/removed (tool-free if possible)

::: {.callout-example}
## Example: Building a Claw

**Step 1**: Build a cardboard prototype. Does it close? Is it strong enough?

**Step 2**: Build the actual claw. Test it 100 times. Does it survive?

**Step 3**: Mount it temporarily. Can you swap it for a different end effector?

**Step 4**: Final integration. Document the mounting points and any code needed.
:::
